nativeRange.habitat: Rapidly flowing streams and rivers with heavy vegetation; also warm springs, weedy canals, and ponds.

decorPreferences: dense planting along sides and back

diet.notes: Readily accepts high-quality flakes, pellets, and gel foods. Benefits from variety including frozen or live bloodworms, brine shrimp, and daphnia; occasional algae wafers or blanched vegetables round out the diet.

summary: A hardy and active livebearer from Mexico and Central America. Males carry a striking sword-shaped caudal extension and tend to spar or harass females — a 1M:2–3F ratio in a 120L+ tank is generally recommended.
careNotes:
Swordtails are generally considered beginner-friendly but benefit from attention to sex ratios: males have been known to relentlessly chase and stress females, so keeping at least two or three females per male is commonly advised. An active, mid-water swimmer that appreciates a long tank footprint (120 cm+) with open swimming space and planted margins.

Aquarium Co-Op and SeriouslyFish agree on a preference for harder, slightly alkaline water (pH 7.0–8.4, gH 10–25). A temperature spread of 19–28°C has been documented; the practical hobbyist sweet spot tends toward 22–26°C.

Males spar with each other — fighting is typically cosmetic rather than lethal, but a single male per tank avoids chronic stress. Multiple males can be kept in larger, well-planted systems.

Swordtails hybridize readily with Platies (X. maculatus) and Variatus Platies (X. variatus) — all belong to genus Xiphophorus. Hybrids are reported to be sterile or semi-fertile. Keeping swordtails alongside either platy species is generally discouraged if line purity matters.

Numerous captive-bred color and fin morphs exist: Red, Velvet, Pineapple, Marigold, Kohaku, Koi, Lyretail, Sailfin, Highfin, and others. Long-finned morphs tend to be more prone to fin rot and bacterial infections.

Escape risk: males in particular have been documented jumping from uncovered tanks — a tight-fitting lid is strongly recommended.

Gestation typically lasts 24–30 days; females may store viable sperm for several months. Fry are large enough to accept finely crushed flake or baby brine shrimp immediately. Dense planting or a breeding box helps protect fry from adult predation.
breedingNotes: Prolific viviparous livebearer. Females can produce 20–200 fry per drop; broods arrive approximately every 4–6 weeks at typical aquarium temperatures. Females have been documented storing viable sperm for months, enabling multiple broods from a single mating. Sexual maturity is reached around 3 months of age. No parental care is provided — adults will eat fry if not separated. Dense floating vegetation or a breeding box is commonly used to protect fry.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Used as primary after Aquarium Co-Op returned 404 for the expected swordtail care page.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adultSizeCm (males 14 cm, females 16 cm — agrees with SeriouslyFish), temperature (22–28°C), pH (7.0–8.0), hardness (9–19 dH). Slight gH discrepancy: FishBase states 9–19 dH vs SeriouslyFish 179–447 ppm (~10–25 dGH) and Fishkeeping World 12–30 dGH. Averaged to {min:10, max:25}.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (15 gal minimum for single; SeriouslyFish recommends 120 cm footprint ~108L), pH (7.0–8.4), lifespan (3–5 years), temperament (peaceful/territorial per male behavior). Tank minimum set at 120L to reflect trio-appropriate sizing per controller guidance.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Partial data retrieved (temperature 21–27°C, pH >7.0, size 10–16 cm, diet notes, hybridization warning with platies). Original care-guide URL returned 404; alternate URL yielded partial content used to corroborate other sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Prolific livebearer; females drop fry every 4–6 weeks. Fry are robust and easily raised. Main challenge is preventing adults from eating fry.
